nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on clear lagoon and seaward reefs from the lower surge zone, with depth records ranging from roughly 0–30 m. Two recognised subspecies exist: O. m. camurum (Hawaiian Islands) and O. m. clippertonense (Eastern Pacific).

diet.notes: Has been documented in the wild feeding on didemnid tunicates, polychaetes, sponges, mollusks, copepods, and algae. In captivity, newly introduced individuals tend to accept live brine shrimp or bloodworms initially; once acclimated, the diet can typically be transitioned to chopped squid, clams, mussels, krill, and frozen herbivore preparations with marine algae.

summary: A small Indo- and Eastern-Pacific boxfish with striking sexual dichromatism — males vivid blue with orange-banded sides, females black with white spots. Generally considered an expert-only species owing to its skin toxin (ostracitoxin) that can prove lethal to tankmates under stress.
careNotes:
The Spotted Boxfish is widely considered suitable only for very experienced marine aquarists. Its most critical trait is its ability to release ostracitoxin from mucous glands when stressed or dying — a toxin that has been reported to kill all other fish in a closed system within a very short period. Extreme care is generally advised to minimise stress during handling, transport, and acclimation; quarantine in a dedicated, well-filtered tank is commonly recommended before introducing it to any display system.

Sexual dichromatism is pronounced: adult males display a vivid blue body with a black cap and orange lateral bands, while females and juveniles are dark brown to black with small white spots. The species is broadly distributed across the Indo- and Eastern Pacific, from East Africa east to Mexico and north to southern Japan and Hawaii.

Sources disagree on minimum tank volume: BlueZoo Aquatics cites 100 gallons (~379 L), LiveAquaria cites 180 gallons (~681 L), and ReefApp suggests 800 L. The averaged figure rounds to approximately 700 L, which has been adopted here in line with the playbook's preference for conservatively-larger tank picks for solitary, territorial fish.

Temperature recommendations also span two ranges: hobby sources (LiveAquaria) suggest 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), while FishBase wild data indicates 24.6–29.3°C. The averaged range of approximately 23.4–27.5°C is used here.

Reef compatibility is mixed across sources: ReefApp lists the species as not reef safe, BlueZoo Aquatics lists it as reef safe with caution, and LiveAquaria notes it nibbles tubeworms. Reef-safe status has therefore been recorded as 'with caution', and the species is generally not considered safe in tanks containing small invertebrates, shrimps, crabs, or delicate corals.

Breeding in captivity has rarely been achieved. The species is not protogynous in the sense documented for wrasses; instead, males defend territories containing one or more females in the wild.
breedingNotes: Very little success has been reported in breeding the Spotted Boxfish in captivity. Wild populations have been observed forming small harem-like groups, with males defending territories that overlap those of one or more females.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (25 cm TL max), wild temperature range (24.6–29.3°C), taxonomy (Ostraciidae / Tetraodontiformes), native range and habitat (0–30 m, lagoon and seaward reefs). FishBase wild temperature range is warmer than hobby-source recommendations; averaged across sources.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Additional cross-check; cites max size 25 cm, minimum tank 800 L, salinity ~1.026, explicitly flags species as not reef safe. Tank size factored into averaged minimum volume.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Additional cross-check; cites 10 inch (~25 cm) max size, 100 gallon (~379 L) minimum tank, reef compatible 'with caution', care level 'expert-only'. Lower tank-size figure factored into averaged minimum volume.

fish.breedingNotes: Very little success has been reported in breeding the Spotted Boxfish in captivity. Wild populations have been observed forming small harem-like groups, with males defending territories that overlap those of one or more females.
